title: A New Turn in the Income-Tax Case: Why, and How?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, omitted by OCR, and repaired `I met` where OCR read the capital `I` as `1`. The spellings `quitely` and the first `envelop` are visibly printed in the source quotation and are deliberately preserved. Other repairs are confined to unambiguous OCR word joins, party abbreviations, and glyph errors. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 30-6-2014, an Income Tax Department case against Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and her close associate Sasikala came before Judge Dakshinamurthy in the First Additional Chief Metropolitan Magistrate Court for Economic Offences at Egmore, Chennai. A new petition filed on Jayalalithaa’s behalf was accepted, and the trial was postponed to 24 July, a full twenty-four days. Every newspaper has reported it.

Sasi Enterprises, in which Jayalalithaa and Sasikala were partners, did not file income-tax returns for 1991-92 and 1992-93. Nor did they submit documents for their individual incomes in 1993-94. The Income Tax Department prosecuted them in 1996. The case has remained pending in the First Economic Offences Court in the Egmore court complex for the past fifteen years.

When it came up on 30-6-2014, Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Sasi Enterprises sought an adjournment. They said they had petitioned the Income Tax Department for departmental settlement of the failure to file returns and that the petition remained pending. Counsel for the Department said it was under consideration. The court therefore postponed the case to the 24th.

Senior advocate Ramasamy, appearing for the Income Tax Department, told reporters: “Petitions have been given to the Director General of Income Tax on behalf of the Chief Minister and Sasikala Natarajan. They state that they are prepared to resolve the income-tax issue by compounding, paying all charges including the tax arrears. It was submitted that the trial could continue after the Department’s action on those petitions became known, and should be adjourned in the meantime. I did not oppose it. The magistrate therefore adjourned the trial.”
On 20-4-2014, I wrote about this case in *Murasoli* under the title “Justice! Are You Still There?”

Jayalalithaa and Sasikala filed petitions seeking discharge from the cases and then delayed pursuing them. The Income Tax Department consequently approached the High Court and Supreme Court. On 4-9-2006, the Supreme Court directed the trial court to decide within four weeks their petition seeking discharge. The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate heard and dismissed it. Thus, nine years after the case was filed in 1997, the accused sought discharge, and that petition too was dismissed.
After the Egmore magistrate rejected the discharge petition, Jayalalithaa’s side appealed to the Madras High Court. That appeal was dismissed on 2-12-2006.

They then appealed the High Court order to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court held that there was no fundamental reason to interfere with the Madras High Court’s order and no merit sufficient to entertain the appeal. On 30-1-2014, it directed the trial court to complete the case within four months.

The case was filed in 1997. The magistrate dismissed the discharge application in 2006, nine years later. The High Court dismissed it on 2-12-2006. Eight years after Jayalalithaa appealed in 2006, the Supreme Court dismissed the appeal on 30-1-2014 and ordered completion of the trial within just four months. Those four months have passed. Has the trial concluded? No.

In that judgment the Supreme Court stated: “The principal question to be decided is whether the burden of proving the charge lies upon the Income Tax Department, which made it, or upon the accused Jayalalithaa and Sasikala. In this case, because an intention on the part of the accused can be inferred, it is they who are obliged to establish beyond doubt that they are innocent. They must therefore prove before the trial court the circumstances in which they failed to file their income-tax returns.”
It must be remembered from this that the burden of proving their innocence lies upon Jayalalithaa and Sasikala.

In the very case in which the Supreme Court wrote all this and directed completion within four months, the same Court later postponed the trial for three months because Jayalalithaa had to campaign in the parliamentary election. Only four days of election campaigning remained at the time. It also ordered Jayalalithaa and Sasikala to appear without fail on the date fixed by the trial court. Was even that order respected? Did they appear on the appointed day?

Here is another episode. The case concerning their failure to file individual income-tax returns for 1993-94 came before Supreme Court Justices B.N. Agrawal and A.K. Mathur on 24-2-2006. Jayalalithaa’s lawyer asked for six weeks.
Do you know what the judges said?

“You are making a mockery of the administration of justice. How much longer can you drag these proceedings? You must appear in court, answer questions under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, and set an example to others. What counter-affidavit can you possibly file in this matter? We wish to issue an order today itself directing your client to appear before the court and answer its questions.”
*The Hindu*, dated 25-2-2006, “Court pulls up Jayalalithaa”:

“You (Jayalalithaa) are making a mockery of the Judicial Process. How long you can drag the proceedings? You must set an example to others by presenting yourself in the court for questioning under section 313 cr. P.C. What counter affidavit you can file in this matter. We want to pass an order today itself directing your client to be present in the Court”

On 20-3-2014, the case came before the same Judge R. Dakshinamurthy in the First Economic Offences Court at Egmore. Because charges had to be framed, he ordered Jayalalithaa and Sasikala to appear in person on 3 April. They did not appear. Their lawyer said Jayalalithaa was electioneering and Sasikala suffered from back pain and diabetes, and sought exemption from personal appearance. Counsel for the Income Tax Department strongly opposed the request.

The magistrate then ordered both to appear on 10 April. Again they did not. Neither appeared when the matter came up on 19 May. It was adjourned to 3 June. On that date, the judge ordered them to attend on 9-6-2014. They did not appear. Judge Dakshinamurthy ordered that they must appear in person for the hearing on 30 June. Was even that order implemented? No.

Jayalalithaa has disregarded court orders in all these ways. It was in her case that yesterday’s hearing took place and was postponed to the 24th. The adjournment was granted because she now says that a petition has been filed for departmental settlement and remains under consideration.

As early as 15-7-1998, *The Statesman* reported: “Following the temporary peace accord Jayalalithaa reached with the Union government, the Vajpayee government has taken steps to release her from the income-tax and other cases against her. Commissioner S.C. Jadhav, who had supervised the cases against Jayalalithaa, was transferred to what was considered an excellent post in Mumbai. K. Gopalan replaced him. P.K. Sridharan, regarded as an officer of integrity, was transferred to another important assignment and N.P. Tripathi appointed in his place.

“Income-tax officials alleged that through such transfers Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and their relatives might obtain temporary relief. By placating Jayalalithaa, the BJP government secured the support it required. In return, the demand that the DMK government in Tamil Nadu be dismissed was temporarily suspended.”

There is more. When the AIADMK was allied with the BJP and participated in the Union government, senior BJP leader and then Finance Minister Yashwant Sinha wrote a book titled *Confessions of a Swadeshi Reformer*. On page 226, he wrote:

“In Chennai, after completing my programme in the forenoon, I quitely left for Jayalalitha’s residence. Fortunately there were no photographers waiting there. I was rushed into the living room, where she waited for me. We chatted for a while, and then went for lunch. There were only three of us for lunch - Jayalalitha, Dilip Roy and myself. The lunch was delicious, served by liveried bearers wearing white gloves; everything was done in great style. As I was about to leave, Jayalalitha handed me an envelop. Later when I opened it, I found it was a note about her income tax cases. I met the Prime Minister a few days later and reported the details of my meeting with Jayalalitha in Chennai but forgot to mention the envelope. After I had finished my narration, Vajpayee innocently asked me about the envelope she had given to me and its contents. I was taken aback. Obviously, the Prime Minister of India gets to know everything, if he so wishes.”

In Tamil: After completing my morning programme in Chennai, I went to Jayalalithaa’s home. Fortunately, no photographers were waiting there. I entered the room where she was, spoke with her for a while and had lunch. Only three of us ate: Jayalalithaa, Dilip Ray, Minister of State for Coal, and I. The meal was good. As I was leaving, Jayalalithaa gave me an envelope. When I opened it later, I found notes about her income-tax cases.

A few days later, I met the Prime Minister and reported the details of my discussion with Jayalalithaa in Chennai, but forgot to mention the envelope. The Prime Minister, Vajpayee, then asked, “What was in the envelope Jayalalithaa gave you?” I was startled. I understood that if the Prime Minister wished, he could learn everything happening in the country.
That is what former Union Finance Minister Yashwant Sinha recorded in his book.

This episode occurred in 1998, when the AIADMK was part of an alliance with the BJP. It too concerned an income-tax case. A former Union Finance Minister himself records that Jayalalithaa invited the Union Finance Minister to her home, served him lunch and gave him an envelope containing her representation.

What is the position now? When Prime Minister Manmohan Singh earlier visited Tamil Nadu, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa did not go to receive him. Today, she goes to the airport to receive and see off the present Prime Minister and speaks with him privately for fifteen minutes. Only afterward does her side inform the Egmore court that it has petitioned the Income Tax Department for settlement and that the petition is under consideration. Counsel for the Department tells reporters that he raised no objection.

What will happen? What will the BJP government do? Will it remember the lesson that the AIADMK previously allied with the BJP, imposed numerous demands and conditions, and ultimately helped bring down the BJP government? Will the BJP and its Prime Minister remember former Prime Minister Vajpayee’s remark, on the day the AIADMK broke with the BJP, that he would finally sleep peacefully that night?
Let us wait and watch this spectacle!
With affection,
M.K.
